We conclude that a cheap signal is a circuit proposal, not a confirmed circuit; closure is what separates them.

AI.tldr: Cheap clustering signals only propose circuits; causal ablation closure tests are needed to confirm functional circuits. Dense model communities passed closure, but MoE model failed - ablation improved loss.
AI.motivation: To determine whether cheap signals from clustering co-activation statistics actually identify functional attention-head circuits, or merely propose them. Need to validate whether discovered communities causally contribute to model function.
AI.method: Adapted sparse-autoencoder clustering to attention heads, then performed closure tests: ablated discovered communities and compared per-example damage to matched-random controls. Applied to dense models (Pythia 1B, OLMo 1B) and Mixture-of-Experts model (OLMoE-1B-7B). Extended analysis across training to examine selectivity and participation ratio.
AI.result: In dense models, communities passed closure (ablation damaged loss as expected). In MoE model, clustering found statistically real signal but failed closure - ablation improved loss (wrong direction). Across training, attention-target selectivity and participation ratio decoupled from function.
AI.conclusion: A cheap signal (like co-activation clustering) provides only circuit proposals, not confirmed circuits. Closure tests (causal ablation that matches or exceeds random controls) are necessary to validate functional circuits. Selectivity and participation metrics alone can decouple from actual function during training.

title: Constrained user-item allocation for e-commerce marketing campaigns
summary: When running marketing campaigns, retailers must decide which products to promote and which users to target. These decisions are inherently coupled: effective campaigns match users and items with strong mutual affinity into non-overlapping groups of predefined sizes. However, existing approaches assume predefined campaign structure or decouple item selection from user assignment, and cannot discover campaign groupings directly from joint interaction patterns. We therefore formalize this campaign problem as auto-targeting: jointly selecting users and items to construct multiple disjoint campaigns. To solve this combinatorial problem, we propose three complementary strategies: (i) constrained spectral biclustering to find dense regions in the user-item affinity matrix, (ii) greedy local search with pairwise swaps for combinatorial refinement, and (iii) a multi-armed bandit framework to escape local optima through exploration. We evaluate these methods on a synthetic dataset, the Amazon Reviews benchmarks, and large-scale proprietary commercial data, and compare the results to simulated annealing as a baseline. The results show that biclustering consistently achieves the highest campaign quality, lift, and fairness scores. While biclustering runs efficiently on smaller datasets, its runtime increases substantially on very large ones, where bandit-based methods instead offer a scalable alternative.

AI.tldr: New auto-targeting approach jointly selects users and items to construct multiple disjoint campaigns using biclustering, greedy search, and bandit methods, outperforming existing approaches in campaign quality.
AI.motivation: Existing campaign optimization approaches either assume predefined campaign structure or decouple item selection from user assignment, failing to discover optimal campaign groupings directly from joint interaction patterns.
AI.method: Three strategies: 1) Constrained spectral biclustering to find dense user-item affinity regions; 2) Greedy local search with pairwise swaps for combinatorial refinement; 3) Multi-armed bandit framework for exploration to escape local optima.
AI.result: On synthetic data, Amazon Reviews benchmarks, and large-scale commercial data, biclustering consistently achieved highest campaign quality, lift, and fairness scores. While efficient on smaller datasets, biclustering runtime increases substantially on very large datasets where bandit methods offer scalable alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Biclustering achieves highest campaign quality but has scalability issues on very large datasets, where bandit methods offer a practical alternative.

title: Data-driven discovery of governing differential equations across physical systems
summary: Differential equations play a critical role in scientific discovery because they provide a mathematical framework to describe the behaviour of physical phenomena. As a promising alternative to traditional first principles, data-driven differential equation discovery has attracted increasing attention for its ability to infer governing laws directly from experimental or simulated data, especially when the underlying physics is unclear. However, the field has expanded rapidly along diverse methodological directions, particularly with the emergence of AI-based approaches, and still lacks a clear organizing perspective. In this Review, we propose a problem-oriented perspective on data-driven differential equation discovery. We first introduce a two-dimensional phase diagram of equation discoverability, where discovery problems are organized according to structural complexity and coefficient complexity. This phase diagram shows how the field has moved from the discovery of sparse equations with simple coefficients toward more complex governing laws with richer structures and more flexible parameterizations. It also clarifies why different methodological families succeed or fail in different problem settings. We then present the representation-evaluation-optimization (REO) framework as a fundamental abstraction of the discovery process. By identifying the core problems of equation discovery that persist across algorithmic variations, REO shifts the discussion from individual algorithms to the fundamental principles that determine discoverability. We connect these perspectives to applications across physics and adjacent sciences, and argue that the next challenge is not merely recovering equations, but using them to revise existing theories, distil mechanisms and form new scientific concepts.

AI.tldr: This review proposes organizing data-driven differential equation discovery through a phase diagram based on structural and coefficient complexity, and introduces the REO framework to abstract the discovery process and shift focus from algorithms to fundamental principles.
AI.motivation: Data-driven differential equation discovery has expanded rapidly with diverse methodological approaches, particularly AI-based methods, but lacks a clear organizing perspective to understand when and why different methods succeed or fail across problem settings.
AI.method: The authors propose two key organizing frameworks: 1) A two-dimensional phase diagram organizing discovery problems by structural complexity and coefficient complexity, and 2) The representation-evaluation-optimization (REO) framework as a fundamental abstraction of the discovery process that shifts focus from individual algorithms to core principles.
AI.result: The phase diagram shows how the field has evolved from discovering sparse equations with simple coefficients toward more complex governing laws with richer structures and flexible parameterizations. The REO framework identifies core problems that persist across algorithmic variations and clarifies why different methodological families succeed in different problem settings.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes a problem-oriented perspective on data-driven differential equation discovery, introducing a phase diagram of equation discoverability and the REO framework as fundamental abstractions. It argues that the next challenge is not just recovering equations but using them to revise theories, distill mechanisms, and form new scientific concepts.

title: A Unifying Framework for Concept-Based Representational Similarity
summary:
Learned representations across models and modalities often exhibit striking structural similarities, suggesting shared underlying concept decompositions. However, concept alignment remains poorly defined: existing approaches optimize different objectives under the same terminology, obscuring what is actually aligned.
  We propose a unifying framework that decomposes alignment along two axes: what is aligned (representations vs. concepts) and at what level (instance-wise vs. distributional). This induces four corresponding properties -- instance-wise and distributional variants of translation and concept consistency -- and reveals precisely which of these guarantees existing methods provide. We further introduce \InterVenchA, an intervention-based benchmark that separately measures extraction quality, translation quality, and concept consistency. Through theory and experiments, we show that commonly assumed equivalences between alignment objectives fail in practice: optimizing one property does not reliably recover the others, and purely unsupervised objectives fail to recover meaningful instance-level alignment. We then propose the Coupled Sparse Autoencoder (CoSAE), which jointly enforces complementary alignment objectives. Strong alignment emerges only in this regime. Surprisingly, as little as 0.1\% paired data is sufficient to recover instance-level alignment when anchoring distributional objectives.
  Overall, our results show that concept alignment is fundamentally multi-objective: it must be defined, measured, and optimized as such.

AI.tldr: We propose a framework to unify concept alignment definitions, show existing methods don't guarantee all alignment properties, and introduce a method that achieves strong alignment with minimal paired data.
AI.motivation: Learned representations across models and modalities often show structural similarities suggesting shared concept decompositions, but concept alignment remains poorly defined with different approaches optimizing different objectives under the same terminology.
AI.method: We propose a unifying framework that decomposes alignment along two axes: representations vs. concepts, and instance-wise vs. distributional. We introduce InterVenchA benchmark and Coupled Sparse Autoencoder (CoSAE) that jointly enforces complementary alignment objectives.
AI.result: Commonly assumed equivalences between alignment objectives fail in practice. Optimizing one property doesn't reliably recover others. Unsupervised objectives fail to recover meaningful instance-level alignment. CoSAE enables strong alignment with just 0.1% paired data sufficient for instance-level alignment when anchoring distributional objectives.
AI.conclusion: Concept alignment is fundamentally multi-objective and must be defined, measured, and optimized as such. Strong alignment emerges only when jointly enforcing complementary alignment objectives, with surprisingly little paired data needed to recover instance-level alignment.

title: Muon Learns More Robust and Transferable Features than Adam
summary: Muon has recently emerged as a state-of-the-art optimizer for pretraining Large Language Models (LLMs) and vision classifiers. Despite its efficiency advantage over Adam and SGD, the feature-learning advantage of Muon remains unclear. This paper investigates Muon's feature-learning advantage through the lens of robustness and transferability. First, by evaluating pretrained models on corrupted images and texts, we show that features learned by Muon are consistently more robust than those learned by Adam and SGD across different architectures, including transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Using trained layer-wise probes, we further show that this robustness advantage is reflected in larger logit margins across layers. Second, by training linear classifiers or fine-tuning full models from pretrained parameters on downstream tasks, we demonstrate that Muon-learned features transfer more effectively than those learned by Adam and SGD. This transferability advantage is further supported by the diversity of hidden states across layers, as measured by effective rank. Finally, in a representative classification problem with multi-component features, we prove that Muon attains larger margins and higher effective rank than Adam and SGD, providing theoretical support for our empirical findings.

AI.tldr: Muon outperforms Adam and SGD in feature-learning: features are more robust to corrupted data and transfer better to downstream tasks, with theoretical support showing larger margins and higher effective rank.
AI.motivation: Despite Muon's known efficiency advantage for pretraining LLMs and vision models, its feature-learning advantages (robustness and transferability) remain unclear compared to optimizers like Adam and SGD.
AI.method: Evaluating pretrained models on corrupted images/texts, training layer-wise probes to analyze logit margins, training linear classifiers/fine-tuning on downstream tasks, measuring hidden state diversity using effective rank, and theoretical analysis of a representative classification problem.
AI.result: Features learned by Muon are consistently more robust to corrupted data across transformers and CNNs, with larger logit margins across layers. Muon-learned features also transfer more effectively to downstream tasks, supported by higher effective rank indicating greater hidden state diversity.
AI.conclusion: Muon demonstrates superior feature-learning capabilities compared to Adam and SGD, achieving better robustness against corrupted data and improved transferability to downstream tasks. This is supported by both empirical evidence across various architectures and theoretical analysis showing larger logit margins and higher effective rank.

title: In-Context Learning for Latent Space Bayesian Optimization
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a central tool for sample-efficient design, and latent-space Bayesian optimization (LSBO) extends it to structured objects such as molecules and proteins. In parallel, tabular foundation models such as TabPFN and TabICL now achieve state-of-the-art regression performance and are increasingly used as BO surrogates. Because their Bayesian behavior is induced by large synthetic pretraining collections, the composition of this pretraining distribution is crucial. LSBO creates a distinctive mismatch: the induced map from latent code to objective value differs markedly from the regression tasks used to train current in-context models. We address this mismatch by complementing the pretraining stage of tabular foundation model surrogates with synthetic optimization tasks defined on the latent space of a molecular VAE. The continued-pretraining objective features a regularizer that anchors the model to the original checkpoint, preserving its broad regression prior while avoiding overspecialization to the adaptation tasks. On held-out molecular optimization benchmarks, the resulting model achieves strong performance, supporting the relevance of LSBO-specific adaptation for in-context surrogates.

AI.tldr: tldr
AI.motivation: motivation
AI.method: method
AI.result: result
AI.conclusion: result

title: Algorithm for Contextual Queueing Bandits with Rate-Optimal Queue Length Regret
summary: Contextual queueing bandits provide a framework for learning to schedule heterogeneous jobs under unknown context-dependent service rates. Under stochastic contexts, existing algorithms achieve $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/4})$ queue length regret, defined as the expected difference between the learner's and oracle's queue lengths at horizon $T$. In this paper, we improve this rate to $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/2})$. The key observation is that random exploration is needed only up to a carefully chosen cutoff round, rather than throughout the entire horizon. We propose CQB-$η$-2, a three-phase algorithm: (i) pure random exploration to construct an initial estimator, (ii) $η$-random exploration combined with a UCB rule to continue learning while maintaining negative drift, and (iii) pure UCB after the exploration cutoff. Our proof decomposes the queue length regret at the cutoff round. Before the cutoff, negative drift suppresses queue length differences caused by suboptimal choices. After the cutoff, the first two phases provide sufficient random exploration samples, ensuring that UCB decisions incur small departure-rate gaps. Combining these two bounds yields queue length regret of order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/2})$. We further prove a minimax lower bound of order $Ω(T^{-1/2})$. The proof constructs two hard instances that are statistically indistinguishable up to the final service decision, and uses a queue-specific coupling argument to convert the resulting testing error into queue length regret. Together, our upper and lower bounds characterize the minimax dependence on the horizon $T$ up to logarithmic factors.

AI.tldr: Contextual queueing bandit algorithm achieves optimal Õ(T^{-1/2}) queue length regret via phased exploration cutoff and matching lower bound proof.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for contextual queueing bandits under stochastic contexts achieve only Õ(T^{-1/4}) queue length regret, leaving a gap to the fundamental limit. The paper aims to improve this rate and characterize the minimax dependence on horizon T.
AI.method: The proposed algorithm CQB-η-2 operates in three phases: (1) pure random exploration for initial estimation, (2) η-random exploration combined with UCB rule to continue learning while maintaining negative drift, and (3) pure UCB after exploration cutoff. The analysis decomposes regret before and after cutoff, using negative drift arguments and statistical concentration.
AI.result: Achieves queue length regret of Õ(T^{-1/2}), matching the proved minimax lower bound of Ω(T^{-1/2}). This establishes the optimal rate up to logarithmic factors.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes the minimax optimal rate of queue length regret in contextual queueing bandits as Θ̃(T^{-1/2}), closing the gap between existing upper bounds and fundamental limits.

title: Transition-Based Digital Twin Modelling for Alzheimer's Disease under Sparse Longitudinal Data
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables. Accepted as a full-length paper at the International Conference on AI in Healthcare (AIiH) 2026
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression is highly heterogeneous and is typically observed through sparse and irregular longitudinal data, posing challenges for prediction and personalised monitoring. Existing machine learning approaches have improved AD prediction using multimodal data, yet often focus on static classification or cohort-level risk estimation, providing limited support for subject-specific modelling and uncertainty-aware reasoning. To address these limitations, we present a personalised digital twin framework for AD prediction and scenario-based analysis using multimodal longitudinal data. The proposed approach integrates complementary modelling strategies to capture clinical transitions and temporal dependencies across visits. Using data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), including cognitive assessments, clinical variables, and MRI-derived phenotypes, the framework predicts cognitive status and diagnostic categories while quantifying predictive uncertainty and enabling patient-specific what-if trajectory analysis. Evaluation on leak-free subject-level splits demonstrates strong performance in score forecasting and diagnosis classification. In this sparse and irregular ADNI setting, transition-based modelling of adjacent visits achieved higher predictive accuracy than the sequence-based branch, suggesting that local transition modelling may be more data-efficient. While sequence models remain valuable for uncertainty-aware trajectory forecasting, local transition modelling offers a more data-efficient and robust predictive strategy. These findings highlight the importance of aligning temporal modelling strategies with clinical data structure and suggest that transition-based digital twin formulations may provide a practical and interpretable approach for personalised disease forecasting in neurodegenerative disorders.

AI.tldr: Personalized digital twin framework for Alzheimer's disease prediction using multimodal longitudinal data shows transition-based modeling outperforms sequence-based approaches in sparse data settings for predictive accuracy and data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer's disease progression is heterogeneous and observed through sparse/irregular longitudinal data. Existing ML approaches focus on static classification or cohort-level risk estimation, lacking support for subject-specific modeling and uncertainty-aware reasoning.
AI.method: A personalized digital twin framework integrating complementary modeling strategies (transition-based and sequence-based) to capture clinical transitions and temporal dependencies across visits using multimodal longitudinal data from ADNI (cognitive assessments, clinical variables, MRI-derived phenotypes).
AI.result: The framework demonstrates strong performance in cognitive score forecasting and diagnosis classification. In sparse/irregular ADNI data, transition-based modeling of adjacent visits achieved higher predictive accuracy than sequence-based modeling, suggesting local transition modeling is more data-efficient while sequence models remain valuable for uncertainty-aware trajectory forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Transition-based digital twin formulations may provide a practical and interpretable approach for personalized disease forecasting in neurodegenerative disorders, highlighting the importance of aligning temporal modeling strategies with clinical data structure.

title: AutoMegaKernel: A Statically-Checked Agent Harness for Self-Retargeting Megakernel Synthesis
comment: 18 pages, 5 figures. Open-source code, data, and agent harness: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/AutoMegaKernel
summary:
AutoMegaKernel (AMK) compiles a HuggingFace Llama-family model into a single persistent cooperative CUDA kernel that runs the whole forward pass in one launch, with no per-model hand-written CUDA. The contribution is the system, not raw speed.
  A frozen schedule-IR validator statically certifies deadlock-freedom and race-freedom via static graph checks (not a mechanized proof), so an unsafe agent-proposed schedule is rejected before launch: across 7,160 adversarial schedules (6,091 unsafe) it had zero false-accepts and accepted all 360 real lowerings. The same source retargets sm_80/sm_90/sm_120 from one codebase, auto-generates correct megakernels for 10 of 10 supported models, and on a real SmolLM2-135M checkpoint reproduces HuggingFace greedy decode token-for-token (perplexity match 2.5e-7). An unattended, agent-drivable autoresearch loop self-improves the megakernel over its own baseline (1.25-1.72x).
  A search-found int8 (W8A16) megakernel beats CUDA-graphed cuBLAS bf16 at batch-1 decode across NVIDIA's datacenter inference fleet: L4 up to 1.33x, the current-gen L40S 1.25-1.27x, A10G up to 1.08x at scale, and the consumer RTX 5090 1.19-1.23x. The ordering is not a clean function of bandwidth (the 864 GB/s L40S beats the 600 GB/s A10G); the divide is inference-class vs training-class. AMK trails cuBLAS on the high-bandwidth training-class A100/H100, where the harness localizes the cross-SM-sync bottleneck; we report the gap plainly. This is a precision-asymmetric (W8A16 vs bf16) comparison at decode position 0; the largest real checkpoint is TinyLlama-1.1B. Code and the harness: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/AutoMegaKernel

AI.tldr: AutoMegaKernel automatically compiles LLMs into single CUDA kernels with safety validation, achieving speedups over cuBLAS on inference GPUs while reproducing exact model outputs.
AI.motivation: To create a system that automatically generates correct, safe megakernels for LLM inference without hand-written CUDA code, addressing the challenges of manual kernel optimization while providing safety guarantees.
AI.method: Compiles HuggingFace Llama-family models into single persistent cooperative CUDA kernels using schedule-IR, with static graph validation for deadlock/race freedom. Uses automated search for kernel optimization and supports precision-asymmetric W8A16 quantization.
AI.result: Generates correct megakernels for 10/10 supported models; achieves 1.08-1.33x speedup over cuBLAS bf16 on inference-class GPUs (L4, L40S, A10G, RTX 5090); reproduces HuggingFace output exactly; self-improves performance by 1.25-1.72x via automated search; validates safety with zero false accepts on 6,091 unsafe schedules.
AI.conclusion: AutoMegaKernel demonstrates the viability of automatic persistent kernel generation for LLMs, achieving competitive performance against optimized libraries while providing safety guarantees through static verification. The approach shows particular promise for inference-class GPUs and demonstrates self-improvement capability through automated search.

title: When Do Local Score Models Extrapolate Across Size? A Diagnostic Theory and Benchmark
summary: Scientific generative modeling often requires size transfer, where models trained on small systems are evaluated on larger ones. While translation-invariant architectures enable this evaluation, we show that architectural locality alone does not guarantee stable size extrapolation. Instead, stable extrapolation is governed by the quasi-locality of the Gaussian-smoothed score. Through Tweedie's formula, far-away perturbations can influence local score components via posterior covariance, meaning a local model succeeds only if its receptive field covers the smoothed score's response range. We formalize this mechanism, proving a size-uniform comparison theorem for local marginals under reverse diffusion. We also introduce Finite-Depth Local Flow (FDLF), a white-box diagnostic benchmark with exact scores, densities, and controllable response ranges. Empirically, we validate the interplay between spatial mixing, smoothed-score quasi-locality, and model receptive fields. Under spatial mixing, the smoothed score remains quasi-local relative to the receptive field, enabling stable extrapolation. Conversely, when spatial mixing weakens, the score's locality rapidly degrades, causing size transfer to fail.

AI.tldr: Stable size extrapolation depends on quasi-locality of Gaussian-smoothed scores, not just architectural locality, with spatial mixing controlling this locality through posterior covariance effects.
AI.motivation: To understand why some translation-invariant architectures succeed at size transfer while others fail, despite having architectural locality that should theoretically enable evaluation on larger systems than trained on.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis formalizing the mechanism of stable extrapolation via a size-uniform comparison theorem for local marginals under reverse diffusion, plus introduction of Finite-Depth Local Flow (FDLF) diagnostic benchmark with exact scores and densities for empirical validation.
AI.result: Empirical validation shows that when spatial mixing is present, the smoothed score remains quasi-local relative to model receptive fields, enabling stable extrapolation. When spatial mixing weakens, score locality degrades rapidly, causing size transfer failure.
AI.conclusion: Stable size extrapolation in scientific generative modeling is governed by the quasi-locality of Gaussian-smoothed scores rather than just architectural locality, with the interplay between spatial mixing, smoothed-score quasi-locality, and model receptive fields being the key determining factor for successful size transfer.

title: BrainSurgery: Reproducible and Reliable Declarative Weight Manipulations for Model Editing and Upcycling
summary: As deep learning models scale, managing, inspecting, and modifying large checkpoints has become increasingly challenging. Researchers often need to alter model weights for layer restructuring, precision casting, low-rank factorization, and architectural debugging, yet these workflows often rely on fragile ad-hoc Python scripts. Here, we introduce BrainSurgery, a tool for robust and reproducible "tensor surgery" on neural network checkpoints, and provide a system demonstration covering four examples and three case studies from model upcycling to LoRA extraction. By abstracting storage formats and memory management, BrainSurgery executes complex transformations through declarative YAML plans. It supports structural modifications, mathematical transformations, and tensor reshaping through expressive regex and structural targeting, while built-in assertions validate tensor shapes, data types, and values to prevent silent errors. We envision that BrainSurgery will provide a strong foundation for future research through its reproducible and validated operations.

AI.tldr: BrainSurgery: A tool for robust tensor surgery on neural network checkpoints using declarative YAML plans, supporting structural modifications and transformations with validation assertions.
AI.motivation: Managing, inspecting, and modifying large deep learning checkpoints has become challenging. Existing workflows rely on fragile ad-hoc Python scripts for weight alterations like layer restructuring, precision casting, low-rank factorization, and architectural debugging.
AI.method: Declarative YAML plans with abstraction of storage formats and memory management, using expressive regex and structural targeting to support structural modifications, mathematical transformations, and tensor reshaping. Built-in assertions validate tensor shapes, data types, and values.
AI.result: BrainSurgery is introduced as a tool for robust and reproducible "tensor surgery" on neural network checkpoints. The paper demonstrates four examples and three case studies ranging from model upcycling to LoRA extraction.
AI.conclusion: The authors envision that BrainSurgery will provide a strong foundation for future research through its reproducible and validated tensor surgery operations.

title: Evaluating the Representation Space of Diffusion Models via Self-Supervised Principles
comment: First two authors contributed equally. Accepted at ICML 2026
summary: Diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable generative capabilities and have also emerged as powerful self-supervised representation learners, yet the connection between these two abilities remains less explored. Drawing inspiration from self-supervised learning (SSL), we introduce a framework for jointly evaluating the representation and generation capabilities of diffusion models. Specifically, we decompose features into invariant and residual components and derive the Invariant Contamination Ratio (ICR), a Fisher-based metric that quantifies how residual variation contaminates invariant signal in feature space. We use this framework to analyze both discriminative and generative behavior of diffusion models. On the representation side, we find that invariance peaks at intermediate noise levels, which also yield the best downstream classification performance. On the generative side, we study how training transitions from genuine generalization to memorization in data-limited regimes, and show that ICR serves as a sensitive training-time indicator of early learning: increasing residual energy along Fisher directions marks the onset of memorization, detectable from training features alone without external evaluators or held-out test sets. Overall, our results show that diffusion models can be monitored from a self-supervised perspective through the geometry of their learned representations.

AI.tldr: A framework connecting diffusion models' representation and generation abilities via feature decomposition and ICR metric, showing invariance peaks at optimal noise levels and providing early detection of memorization.
AI.motivation: To explore the connection between diffusion models' generative capabilities and their potential as self-supervised representation learners, and to develop a framework for jointly evaluating both abilities.
AI.method: The framework decomposes features into invariant and residual components and derives the Invariant Contamination Ratio (ICR), a Fisher-based metric that quantifies how residual variation contaminates invariant signal in feature space.
AI.result: Invariance peaks at intermediate noise levels, correlating with best downstream classification performance; ICR serves as a training-time indicator that detects memorization onset through increased residual energy along Fisher directions.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models can be effectively monitored through self-supervised analysis of their learned representations, with the Invariant Contamination Ratio serving as a sensitive training-time indicator that reveals the transition from generalization to memorization.

title: Preserving Plasticity in Continual Learning via Dynamical Isometry
summary: Continual training of deep neural networks under non-stationarity often leads to a progressive loss of plasticity, eventually limiting further learning. We relate plasticity to the empirical Neural Tangent Kernel, and identify dynamical isometry (the condition that layer-wise Jacobian singular values remain close to one) as a key mechanism for preserving plasticity in continual learning. We revisit a class of networks that are almost-everywhere isometric while remaining universal Lipschitz function approximators, demonstrating that near-dynamical isometry is compatible with expressive nonlinear representations. For general architectures, we propose an efficient isometry-promoting regularization scheme and identify a novel mechanism by which it can reactivate dormant ReLU units. Building on this, we introduce AdamO, an Adam-style adaptive optimizer that decouples isometry regularization from gradient updates, analogous to AdamW. We further reinterpret prior plasticity-preserving approaches through the lens of dynamical isometry, showing that they target only a partial measure of isometry. Across supervised and reinforcement-learning continual-learning benchmarks designed to induce plasticity loss, our methods consistently match or outperform existing approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies dynamical isometry as crucial for preserving plasticity in continual learning, proposes an efficient regularization scheme and AdamO optimizer, and demonstrates superior performance on continual learning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the progressive loss of plasticity in deep neural networks during continual training under non-stationary conditions, which limits further learning.
AI.method: The method involves: 1) Relating plasticity to the empirical Neural Tangent Kernel and identifying dynamical isometry as key; 2) Revisiting networks that are almost-everywhere isometric; 3) Proposing an efficient isometry-promoting regularization scheme; 4) Introducing AdamO, an Adam-style optimizer that decouples isometry regularization; 5) Reinterpreting prior plasticity-preserving approaches through the dynamical isometry lens.
AI.result: Results show that: 1) Dynamical isometry helps preserve plasticity in continual learning; 2) Near-dynamical isometry is compatible with expressive nonlinear representations; 3) The proposed isometry regularization can reactivate dormant ReLU units; 4) AdamO consistently matches or outperforms existing approaches across supervised and reinforcement-learning continual-learning benchmarks designed to induce plasticity loss.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that dynamical isometry is a key mechanism for preserving plasticity in continual learning, and the proposed AdamO optimizer with isometry regularization effectively maintains plasticity and outperforms existing approaches across various benchmarks.

title: Disentanglement with Holographic Reduced Representations
summary: Disentanglement, the separation of factors of variation in data using neural networks, remains a long-standing challenge in machine learning. Prior work has addressed this problem with variational autoencoders and generative adversarial networks that incorporate ideas from variational inference and information-theoretic constraints. In contrast to methods that rely on continuous representations, we propose a design that treats disentangled representations as symbolic structures, motivated by the compositional relationships among the concepts that make up samples from a distribution. However, learning discrete symbolic structures with neural networks while maintaining differentiability is difficult and often requires complex architectures. To address this, we introduce an unsupervised learning algorithm that uses holographic reduced representations (HRR) for neural disentanglement. We show that the HRR unbinding operation provides an inductive bias for separating factors and yields competitive results against baselines, as measured by latent traversals and disentanglement metrics. We complement these empirical findings with an information-theoretic analysis of the HRR unbinding channel. We prove that unbinding induces approximately independent symbol-value pairs and derive a per-slot capacity bound that quantifies how many distinct symbolic concepts can be reliably encoded, giving a quantitative account of the inductive bias toward disentanglement. The resulting representations differ from standard autoencoder-based models, in that their latent units are vectors that are summed together, rather than scalar dimensions of a low-dimensional latent vector. We show that this HRR representation is more robust to noise than other disentangled representations and maintains reconstruction quality across a range of SNRs.

AI.tldr: Proposes using holographic reduced representations (HRR) for neural disentanglement, treating factors as symbolic structures rather than continuous dimensions. Shows competitive performance and robustness to noise.
AI.motivation: Existing disentanglement methods rely on continuous representations, but disentangled factors often correspond to symbolic, compositional concepts. Learning discrete symbolic structures with neural networks while maintaining differentiability is challenging.
AI.method: Using holographic reduced representations (HRR) with unbinding operations to create symbolic structures for disentanglement. The approach treats representations as compositional symbolic concepts rather than continuous latent dimensions.
AI.result: HRR-based method achieves competitive disentanglement performance measured by latent traversals and metrics. It induces approximately independent symbol-value pairs, provides quantifiable capacity bounds for encoded concepts, and shows robustness to noise while maintaining reconstruction quality across SNRs.
AI.conclusion: Holographic reduced representations provide an effective inductive bias for neural disentanglement, offering competitive performance against continuous representation baselines while maintaining differentiability and robustness to noise.

title: Bandits for Efficient Experimentation: Adapting to Control Group, Preferences, and Context Drifts
summary: We consider a variant of the linear contextual stochastic multi-armed bandits, where the learner must provide recommendations to a group of users, each having its personalized preference vector, and in the presence of context distributions that are drifting over time. Under practitioner-friendly assumptions, we reduce this setting to linear bandit with stationary mean but heteroskedastic and non-stationary noise. We further study the case when the learner must ensure the mean reward of each decision must exceed that of a baseline strategy $\boldsymbolπ_0$ at each decision step. We introduce Dri-MED, an algorithm inspired from the linear version of the MED strategy, and carefully adapted to handle the non-stationary heteroskedastic noise. We show that the instance-dependent regret scales as $\tilde{\mathcal O}\left(\fracκ{\tildeΔ}d^2(\log(T)\right)$, where $\tildeΔ$ is the constraint-aware sub-optimality gap subject to policy $π_0$, with variance-aware multiplicative term $κ$ that we carefully handle using heteroskedastic regression. We further show Dri-MED enjoys $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ expected constraint violations. Our numerical results suggest that Dri-MED significantly outperforms conservative baselines that ignores the drift and preference structure.

AI.tldr: Contextual bandits with user preferences, context drift, and per-step constraints solved via Dri-MED algorithm achieving improved regret bounds.
AI.motivation: Address contextual bandits with: 1) personalized user preferences, 2) drifting context distributions over time, 3) constraints requiring each decision's reward to exceed a baseline strategy.
AI.method: Dri-MED algorithm: Adaptation of linear MED strategy to handle non-stationary heteroskedastic noise and constraints.
AI.result: Instance-dependent regret scales as Õ(κ/Δ̃ d² log(T)) with Õ(d) expected constraint violations. Numerical results show Dri-MED significantly outperforms conservative baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dri-MED effectively addresses contextual bandits with user preferences, context drift, and per-step constraints, demonstrating theoretical and empirical superiority over baseline methods.

title: Tight Sample Complexity of Transformers
summary: We tightly characterize the VC dimension of depth-$L$ Transformers with a total of $W$ parameters, mapping an input sequence of length $T$ to a single output, establishing an upper bound of $O(L W \log (T W))$ and a nearly matching lower bound of $Ω(L W \log (T W / L))$. We further tightly characterize the sample complexity of chain-of-thought learning using such a Transformer, showing teacher forcing (i.e. selecting a predictor consistent with the entire chain-of-thought on training data) learns with sample complexity $O\left(L W \log \left(\left(T+T^{\prime}\right) W\right)\right)$ and that any learning rule that uses chain-of-thought data requires at least $Ω\left(L W \log \left(\left(T+T^{\prime}\right) W / L\right)\right)$ examples, where $T$ is the input length and $T^{\prime}$ is the number of autoregressive steps.

AI.tldr: Tight VC dimension bounds for Transformers and chain-of-thought learning, showing how depth, parameters, and sequence length affect learning capacity.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental expressivity and sample complexity of Transformers and chain-of-thought learning mechanisms, particularly how architectural parameters (depth, width, sequence length) affect their learning capacity.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using VC dimension theory to derive upper and lower bounds for depth-L Transformers and chain-of-thought learning with teacher forcing.
AI.result: For depth-L Transformers: VC dimension upper bound O(LW log(TW)) and lower bound Ω(LW log(TW/L)). For chain-of-thought learning: sample complexity O(LW log((T+T')W)) with teacher forcing and lower bound Ω(LW log((T+T')W/L)) for any learning rule.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes tight VC dimension bounds for Transformers and chain-of-thought learning, providing fundamental insights into the expressivity and sample complexity of these architectures.

title: Topological Neural Operators
summary: We introduce Topological Neural Operators (TNOs), a principled framework for operator learning on cell complexes that lifts neural operators (NOs) from functions on points and/or edges to topological domains. TNOs represent data as features defined on cells of varying dimension and model their interactions through Discrete Exterior Calculus, enabling explicit cross-dimensional coupling via gradient-, curl-, and divergence-type operators. The key design principle is to decouple where information flows, as governed by fixed topological operators, from how it is transformed (which is learned), yielding models that respect the geometric support of physical quantities and expose conservation and compatibility structure. We further propose Hierarchical TNOs (HTNOs), which incorporate learned coarse complexes to propagate long-range and topology-dependent information. Our framework subsumes existing NOs as a special case, providing a unified perspective on operator learning across discretizations. Across a range of PDE benchmarks, including irregular-geometry flow problems, TNOs and HTNOs improve accuracy; controlled studies further isolate the benefits of native higher-rank and topological structure. Project page: https://circle-group.github.io/research/TNO

AI.tldr: TNOs lift neural operators to topological domains using Discrete Exterior Calculus for cross-dimensional coupling, with HTNOs adding hierarchical structure for long-range propagation.
AI.motivation: Current neural operators are limited to functions on points and/or edges, lacking a principled way to handle higher-dimensional topological domains and cross-dimensional interactions needed for many physical systems.
AI.method: TNOs represent data as features on cells of varying dimension and model interactions through Discrete Exterior Calculus with explicit cross-dimensional coupling via gradient-, curl-, and divergence-type operators. HTNOs incorporate learned coarse complexes for long-range propagation.
AI.result: TNOs and HTNOs improve accuracy across PDE benchmarks including irregular-geometry flow problems. The framework subsumes existing neural operators as a special case and provides unified perspective on operator learning across discretizations.
AI.conclusion: Topological Neural Operators provide a principled framework for operator learning on cell complexes that respects geometric support of physical quantities and reveals underlying conservation and compatibility structures, unifying various operator learning approaches.

title: Learning Dynamics Reveal a Hierarchy of Weight-Induced Layerwise Gram Metrics
summary: We study feed-forward ReLU networks with fixed readout and quadratic loss. The aim is to rewrite gradient descent not primarily as a dynamics in weight space, but as a collective dynamics closed in terms of fields defined on the training-set space. For a single hidden layer, the weight variables can be eliminated from the activation dynamics, yielding a closed equation for the residuals governed by a collective kernel that factorizes into an input-geometric matrix and a dynamical co-activation matrix. For deeper networks, the residual dynamics retains a clean layer-wise kernel structure. However, from depth three onward, closure requires a hierarchy of weight-induced Gram operators that mediate information transport across layers.

AI.tldr: Reformulating gradient descent for ReLU networks using training-set fields yields closed residual equations: single-layer networks decouple completely via factorized kernels, while deeper networks require hierarchical Gram operators to manage inter-layer information.
AI.motivation: To develop an alternative perspective on gradient descent in neural networks that moves beyond weight-space dynamics by focusing on collective fields defined on the training data space.
AI.method: The analysis involves reformulating gradient descent dynamics for feed-forward ReLU networks with fixed readout using fields defined on training-set space rather than weight space. For single hidden layers, weights are eliminated to obtain a closed residual equation governed by a collective kernel. For deeper networks, the approach preserves layer-wise kernel structure but introduces hierarchical weight-induced Gram operators to achieve closure.
AI.result: For single hidden layer networks, a closed equation for residuals is derived using a collective kernel factorized into input-geometric and dynamical co-activation matrices. For deeper networks, residual dynamics maintains layer-wise kernel structure but requires hierarchical Gram operators for closure starting from depth three.
AI.conclusion: While single-layer networks allow for a complete decoupling of weights via a closed residual equation governed by factorized kernels, deeper networks (from depth three onward) require hierarchical Gram operators to capture inter-layer information transport.

title: An Agency-Transferring Model-Free Policy Enhancement Technique
summary:
Training reinforcement learning (RL) policies from scratch is
  costly: it requires careful reward and environment design,
  extensive tuning, and substantial computation.
  Yet many control problems already have a functional but
  suboptimal policy available as a baseline.
  This paper proposes a method for embedding such a baseline into
  the RL training process, simultaneously improving training
  efficiency relative to from-scratch methods and producing a
  learning policy that outperforms the baseline.
  At each step, the method arbitrates between the baseline policy
  and a trainable learning policy, initially relying strongly on
  the baseline policy and then progressively transferring agency to
  the learning policy.
  By the end of training, the learning policy is a standalone
  neural network that operates without baseline policy support.
  The paper formalizes what it means for the baseline policy to be
  functional: under this policy, the agent reaches a goal set and
  remains there with high probability.
  The proposed arbitration mechanism is designed to exploit this
  property during training, yielding high goal-reaching rates right
  from the beginning of training.
  A theoretical analysis provides a formal interpretation of this
  behavior under stated assumptions and extends it to the final
  baseline-free regime, where explicit lower bounds are derived for
  the goal-reaching probability of the standalone learning policy.
  Empirical results on continuous-control benchmarks show that the
  proposed method achieves returns that match or exceed those of
  competitive approaches, while maintaining the highest
  goal-reaching rates throughout training among the compared
  methods -- including in the final stage, where the learning policy
  operates without any baseline support.

AI.tldr: Method embeds baseline policies into RL training via progressive arbitration, improving efficiency over from-scratch training while producing a standalone learning policy that outperforms the baseline.
AI.motivation: Training RL policies from scratch is costly, but many control problems already have functional but suboptimal baseline policies available.
AI.method: At each step, the method arbitrates between the baseline policy and a trainable learning policy, initially relying strongly on the baseline and progressively transferring agency to the learning policy until it operates standalone.
AI.result: Empirical results show the method matches or exceeds returns of competitive approaches while maintaining the highest goal-reaching rates throughout training, including in the final baseline-free stage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method with baseline arbitration successfully improves training efficiency and produces a policy that outperforms the baseline while maintaining high goal-reaching rates throughout training, including in the final baseline-free regime.

title: Perturbative Contrastive Physical Learning
summary: Responses to perturbations are key to understanding physical systems. The ability to contrast such responses by comparing how a system reacts under slightly different conditions provides a mechanism for learning. Here, we introduce Perturbative Contrastive Physical Learning (PCPL), a general framework in which learning emerges from measurable contrasts between physical states produced by controlled changes to inputs, boundary conditions, parameters, or interpreter functions. PCPL unifies and extends prior approaches: Equilibrium Propagation is rooted in contrasts between free and nudged equilibria in energy-based systems, while Frequency Propagation corresponds to contrasts extracted from sinusoidally driven, frequency-demodulated responses. We show that contrast-driven updates can reflect either local sensitivities or global inverse-problem structure, yet do not require centralized gradient computation. Instead, effective learning geometry emerges implicitly from the system's own physical response, allowing learning behavior to arise without an external processor or explicit backpropagation. We demonstrate PCPL in two platforms: (i) spring networks that update bond stiffness using measured displacements and forces, and (ii) continuous-variable photonic circuits trained via x quadrature measurements and finite-difference estimates of the Jacobian. Both platforms successfully learn classification tasks. We further show that a continuous-variable photonic circuit can be trained to implement analog multiplication, illustrating a step toward more autonomous physical learning systems.

AI.tldr: PCPL enables learning by contrasting perturbed physical responses, eliminating need for centralized gradients.
AI.motivation: To develop a learning mechanism that leverages physical system responses without external processors or explicit backpropagation.
AI.method: Learning via measurable contrasts between physical states induced by controlled perturbations, using displacement/force measurements in spring networks and x quadrature measurements/Jacobian estimates in photonic circuits.
AI.result: Successful learning of classification tasks and analog multiplication in photonic circuits, validating the framework.
AI.conclusion: PCPL offers a general framework for physical learning driven by contrasts in system responses, enabling autonomous learning without centralized gradient computation.

title: iOSWorld: A Benchmark for Personally Intelligent Phone Agents
summary: A useful phone agent needs to be personally intelligent. It should reason over a user's identity, history, and preferences as they exist on the device, not just follow isolated instructions in an impersonal sandbox. Existing mobile agent benchmarks lack this kind of personalization. We introduce iOSWorld, the first interactive native iOS simulator benchmark built around a persistent user identity spanning 26 newly built iOS apps. These apps contain connected data such as transactions, messages, travel records, social relationships, and financial activity. iOSWorld includes 133 tasks across three increasingly difficult categories. Single-app tasks (27) test one app, multi-app tasks (60) span 2 to 8 apps, and memory and personalization tasks (46) require agents to infer patterns from personal data. We evaluate frontier and open-source computer-use models in both vision-only and privileged vision+XML settings. The best configuration reaches 52\% overall but only 37\% on multi-app tasks. Privileged vision+XML access improves frontier models by up to 26 percentage points, while smaller models do not benefit from added accessibility-tree input. We release iOSWorld as an open-source benchmark with all apps, seeded data, tasks, rubrics, and evaluation code.

AI.tldr: iOSWorld is the first interactive iOS simulator benchmark with persistent user identity across 26 apps to test mobile agents' personal intelligence. With 133 tasks across three difficulty levels, it reveals large performance gaps - frontier models achieve 52% overall but only 37% on multi-app tasks.
AI.motivation: Current mobile agent benchmarks lack personalization - they don't test agents' ability to reason over a user's identity, history, and preferences as they exist on real devices. Phone agents need to be personally intelligent rather than just following isolated instructions in an impersonal sandbox.
AI.method: Built an interactive native iOS simulator benchmark with 26 newly developed iOS apps containing connected personal data (transactions, messages, travel records, etc.). Created 133 tasks across three difficulty categories: single-app tasks (27), multi-app tasks spanning 2-8 apps (60), and memory/personalization tasks requiring inference from personal data (46). Evaluated frontier and open-source computer-use models in both vision-only and vision+XML accessibility tree settings.
AI.result: The best configuration achieved 52% overall accuracy but only 37% on multi-app tasks. Privileged vision+XML access improved frontier models by up to 26 percentage points, while smaller models did not benefit from added accessibility-tree input. Significant performance gaps were observed, especially for tasks requiring personalization across multiple apps.
AI.conclusion: iOSWorld addresses the need for personal intelligence in mobile agents by providing the first interactive iOS simulator benchmark with persistent user identity, offering a comprehensive testbed that reveals significant performance gaps in current models, especially for multi-app tasks requiring personalization.

title: Zero Touch Predictive Orchestration: Automating Time-Series Models for the Cloud-Edge Continuum
summary: The Cloud-Edge Continuum (CEC) enables latency-critical applications by distributing resources to the far edge, but its extreme volatility makes proactive Zero Touch Management via time-series forecasting essential. However, orchestrators face a severe "cold start" problem: newly discovered nodes lack the historical data required to train localized predictive models, while generalized models fail to capture unique hardware and microservice behaviors. To solve this, we propose a fully automated time-series prediction architecture driven by a novel data-mixing methodology. At the infrastructure level, we introduce a lightweight, technology-agnostic Resource Exposer (RE) that dynamically discovers nodes and continuously collects customizable telemetry (e.g., compute, network, energy). To overcome the sparsity of these initial local samples, our framework automatically merges them with TimeTrack, our publicly available, high-resolution dataset collected at 45-second intervals. This synergizes TimeTrack's foundational, high-frequency temporal patterns with the precise calibration of the local node data. Processed through a Neural Architecture Search (NAS) engine, the system automatically generates highly accurate baseline models. Experimental results demonstrate that merging the target data with TimeTrack effectively mitigates the cold start challenge. This integration significantly improves forecasting accuracy measured in Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) and accelerates convergence compared to training on the sparse local samples alone, training solely on generic datasets, or mixing the target data with standard alternative datasets, establishing a robust foundation for continuous MLOps deployment.

title: Rethinking the Divergence Regularization in LLM RL
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a key component of post-training large language models (LLMs). In practice, LLM RL is often off-policy because of training-inference mismatch and policy staleness, making trust-region control essential for stable optimization. Mainstream methods such as PPO and GRPO approximate this control with a ratio-clipping mechanism, but the importance ratio can be a poor proxy for distributional shift in long-tailed vocabularies. Recent work such as DPPO addresses this mismatch by replacing ratio-based clipping with a divergence-based mask, yielding a trust region defined by the sampled token's absolute probability shift. However, DPPO still relies on a hard mask: once a token crosses the trust-region boundary in a harmful direction, its gradient is discarded rather than corrected. To address this, we propose Divergence Regularized Policy Optimization (DRPO), which replaces the hard mask with a smooth advantage-weighted quadratic regularizer on policy shift. DRPO preserves the same trust-region geometry as DPPO while inducing bounded, continuous gradient weights that attenuate diverging updates and provide corrective signals beyond the boundary. Experiments across model scales, architectures, and precision settings show that DRPO improves the stability and efficiency of LLM RL training.

AI.tldr: DRPO replaces DPPO's hard masking with smooth regularization for better trust-region control in LLM RL, enhancing training stability.
AI.motivation: Existing trust-region methods for off-policy LLM RL (PPO, GRPO) use poor importance ratio proxies; newer DPPO uses hard masking which discards rather than corrects harmful updates beyond the trust boundary.
AI.method: Replace DPPO's hard mask with a smooth advantage-weighted quadratic regularizer on policy shift, providing continuous gradient attenuation for out-of-trust-region updates.
AI.result: DRPO improves stability and efficiency of LLM RL training across various model scales, architectures, and precision settings compared to previous methods.
AI.conclusion: DRPO introduces a smoother regularization approach for trust-region control in LLM RL, outperforming previous methods in stability and efficiency across diverse model configurations.
